EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pressure trough remained over Maryland on the 12th while an upper-level disturbance passed through in a zonal flow aloft. The forcing from these two systems were responsible for the development of showers and thunderstorms. As thunderstorms passed across the Baltimore metropolitan area during the afternoon locally heavy rainfall of 1 to 2 inches produced minor street flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported along parts of Gwynns Falls Parkway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level disturbance and its associated cold front moved into Maryland on the 25th. Hot and humid conditions ahead of the front led to high amounts of instability. Showers and thunderstorms developed along and ahead of the cold front. Due to the high amounts of instability, thunderstorms were able to mix down strong winds from aloft causing significant amounts of damage across portions of Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A concrete grounded fence was blown down on Fenton Street between Wayne Avenue and Bonifant Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent area of low pressure strengthened over the central portion of the nation on the fifth of February. The storm system slowly moved through the Mid-Atlantic during the night of the 5th before redeveloping off the Mid-Atlantic coast on 6th. The storm system finally moved away from the area on the night of the 6th. ||Strong high pressure continued to pump in plenty of cold air across the region for the entire event. Due to the slow movement of the storm, there was a prolonged period of precipitation. The storm system ushered in copious amounts of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean. The deep moisture combined with the forcing from the storm system to bring a period of heavy precipitation to the area on the night of the 5th through the daytime hours on the 6th. Precipitation finally ended during the evening hours of the 6th as the storm system moved away. Most of the precipitation fell in the form of snow due to the cold air that was already in place.  ||Major snow accumulations were reported throughout the state of Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts over 35 mph were reported across portions of Anne Arundel county. The wind combined with visibility below one quarter of a mile due to snow and blowing snow to cause blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Only 3 days after an historic snow storm hit the region, another major storm blasted portions of western Pennyslvania, northern West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland, and eastern Ohio from the afternoon of February 9th to the early morning of February 11th. Blizzard conditions occurred in Garrett county Maryland, Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia, and in the higher elevations of Fayette and Westmoreland counties in Pennsylvania. Over 2 feet of snow fell across Garrett, Preston and Tucker counties with 9 to 24 inches of snow in Fayette and Westmoreland counties. Snowfall amounts ranged from 28 inches at Red House, MD to 26 inches at Davis, WV, to 19 inches in Farmington, PA. Winds gusting over 35 MPH in the higher elevations caused whiteout conditions at times with visibilities below one quarter mile for an extended period. Elsewhere in western Pennsylvania snowfall was from 8 to 12 inches generally in and north of Pittsburgh to north of I-80. An isolated area of heavier snow also fell in and around New Philadelphia in Tuscarawas county OH, with 8 inches of snow in New Cumberland, OH. In the remainder of eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia 3 to 5 inches of snow fell.

A potent area of low pressure tracked through the Midwest on the 9th. As the low approached the Mid-Atlantic coast, this system phased with energy in the southern branch of the jet stream to cause strong low pressure to develop just off the Delmarva peninsula. The low continued to rapidly strengthen as it moved off to our northeast on the 10th. ||The system spread snow across Maryland that began on the 9th and lasted into the 10th. Major snow accumulations were reported with this storm, especially across the northeastern portion of Maryland. As the low pressure rapidly intensified, strong winds caused blowing and drifting snow that led to blizzard conditions across portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 13.1 inches near Bowie. Snowfall amounts between 7 and 12 inches were reported across the rest of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent area of low pressure tracked through the Midwest on the 9th. As the low approached the Mid-Atlantic coast, this system phased with energy in the southern branch of the jet stream to cause strong low pressure to develop just off the Delmarva peninsula. The low continued to rapidly strengthen as it moved off to our northeast on the 10th. ||The system spread snow across Maryland that began on the 9th and lasted into the 10th. Major snow accumulations were reported with this storm, especially across the northeastern portion of Maryland. As the low pressure rapidly intensified, strong winds caused blowing and drifting snow that led to blizzard conditions across portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be over 35 mph along with visibility below one quarter mile to produce blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low passed through the Mid-Atlantic on the night of the 24th. The upper-level low combined with energy in the southern branch of the jet stream, causing low pressure to rapidly intensify over New England on the 25th into the 26th. Low pressure remained nearly stationary before finally weakening and moving off to the east on the 28th. ||A northwest flow around the low triggered a prolonged period of upslope snow for locations along and west of the Allegheny Front. Gusty winds and locally heavy snow reduced visibility to near zero at times, especially on the night of the 25th into the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 8.8 inches near Frostburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent area of low pressure strengthened over the central portion of the nation on the fifth of February. The storm system slowly moved through the Mid-Atlantic during the night of the 5th before redeveloping off the Mid-Atlantic coast on 6th. The storm system finally moved away from the area on the night of the 6th. ||Strong high pressure continued to pump in plenty of cold air across the region for the entire event. Due to the slow movement of the storm, there was a prolonged period of precipitation. The storm system ushered in copious amounts of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean. The deep moisture combined with the forcing from the storm system to bring a period of heavy precipitation to the area on the night of the 5th through the daytime hours on the 6th. Precipitation finally ended during the evening hours of the 6th as the storm system moved away. Most of the precipitation fell in the form of snow due to the cold air that was already in place.  ||Major snow accumulations were reported throughout the state of Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were estimated to be around 25 to 30 inches across central and eastern Allegany County.

Moderate to high amounts of instability was present due to relatively colder air aloft and warm, moist air at the surface. This caused thunderstorms to become severe with large hail being the primary threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported near Prince Frederick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level low slowly tracked through the central portion of the country on the 25th. A southwest flow ahead of this system ushered in warm and humid air. A cold front dropped into the region from the northeast during the afternoon and evening hours of the 25th. The cold front combined with plenty of instability for showers and thunderstorms to develop. Due to plenty of cold air aloft, thunderstorms produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was reported in Burkittsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure over the Great Lakes slowly tracked to our north on the 8th. The cold front associated with the low moved into the region during the afternoon and evening hours. A moist southerly flow ahead of the front provided instability. The instability combined with the forcing from the front to trigger showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours of the 8th. Due to strong winds aloft, some thunderstorms became severe with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust was estimated around 60 mph near Glenn Burnie Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a deep flow of tropical moisture spreading northward along the east coast, and a near stationary frontal boundary over the region resulted in heavy rain across portions of Lower Maryland from Wednesday morning, September 29th, into Thursday night September 30th. Rainfall amounts ranged from three to seven inches over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts of two to seven inches occurred across the county. Blackwater reported 5.74 inches of rain. Toddville reported 5.65 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure off the East Coast pumped in warm and moist air over Maryland. An upper-level disturbance passed through in the westerly flow aloft during the evening of the 11th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 mph was estimated by a Mariner near Magothy Beach Road and Riverside Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure aloft along with a southwest flow around surface high pressure resulted in hot and humid conditions during the Fourth of July through the 8th. Max temperatures were in the 90s and 100s across most locations. The hottest days were on the 6th and 7th of July where most locations soared past 100 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fatality was discovered due to the heat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front passed through Maryland on the 14th. Warm and moist air ahead of the boundary caused limited amounts of instability. The strong forcing from the front combined with the instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. An isolated thunderstorm across lower southern Maryland produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were down along Hungry Road. There was also damage to a porch roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and cold front moved out of the Ohio Valley into a very warm and relatively moist atmosphere in the Mid-Atlantic. Thunderstorms developed along this front as well as a lee trough during the afternoon. Instability, steep lapse rates, and moderate shear aided in some thunderstorms becoming severe, producing damaging winds during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees down near the intersection of Governors Bridge Road and U.S. 301.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure and strong southerly winds allowed water to pool in the Chesapeake Bay and be pushed onshore. Water was also pushed upstream the tidal Potomac River. Coastal flooding was observed along the lower tidal Potomac River and across the Chespeake Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Harve de Grace:  |Parking lot inundated by 6 inches of water at Tydings Park.  The main pier of Jean Roberts Park was under water.  Water approached within 2 feet of the Seneca Point Condos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure and strong southerly winds allowed water to pool in the Chesapeake Bay and be pushed onshore. Water was also pushed upstream the tidal Potomac River. Coastal flooding was observed along the lower tidal Potomac River and across the Chespeake Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gage measurements and reports indicated moderate coastal flooding occurred.  In Bowley's Quarters, Miller's Island, and Turners, water up to 2 feet deep inundated streets and approached houses.  At North Point, North Point Blvd. was closed between Trappe Road and Battle Grove Road.  Bayside Drive and Seabright Drive was closed due to tidal inundation as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system moved along a boundary situated over east central Ohio, southern Pennsylvania and Northern West Virginia. Severe thunderstorms across eastern Ohio, southwest Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland produced widespread wind damage with 50 thousand homes left without power. The counties that were hardest hit were Belmont and Jefferson counties in Ohio, where numerous large trees blocked roadways and three large radio station towers collapsed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported numerous trees, power poles and power lines down near Accident.

Thunderstorms developed along this front as well as a lee trough during the afternoon. Instability, steep lapse rates, and moderate shear aided in some thunderstorms becoming severe, producing damaging winds during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were down at Madonna Road and Cox Road 3 miles northwest of Jarrettsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system moved along a boundary situated over east central Ohio, southern Pennsylvania and Northern West Virginia. Severe thunderstorms across eastern Ohio, southwest Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland produced widespread wind damage with 50 thousand homes left without power. The counties that were hardest hit were Belmont and Jefferson counties in Ohio, where numerous large trees blocked roadways and three large radio station towers collapsed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported numerous trees, power poles and power lines down in Friendsville.

Thunderstorms developed along this front as well as a lee trough during the afternoon. Instability, steep lapse rates, and moderate shear aided in some thunderstorms becoming severe, producing damaging winds during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were down in Long Green.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and cold front moved out of the Ohio Valley into a very warm and relatively moist atmosphere in the Mid-Atlantic. Thunderstorms developed along this front as well as a lee trough during the afternoon. Instability, steep lapse rates, and moderate shear aided in some thunderstorms becoming severe, producing damaging winds during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were down on Baldwin Mill Road south of Sweet Air Road and on Long Green Pike north of Hydes Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and cold front moved out of the Ohio Valley into a very warm and relatively moist atmosphere in the Mid-Atlantic. Thunderstorms developed along this front as well as a lee trough during the afternoon. Instability, steep lapse rates, and moderate shear aided in some thunderstorms becoming severe, producing damaging winds during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large trees downed and fell onto powerlines at the corner of Muddy Creek Road and Cumberstone Road 1 mile northwest of Galesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system moved northward up the eastern seaboard along a north-to-south oriented stationary front. Very moist low level atmosphere and 70 knot southeasterly winds combined with modest instability to produce damaging winds in the strongest thunderstorms that developed.  An isolated tornado also touched down in Anne Arundel County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees down along and to the east of Route 235.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early in the last week of September, a rich plume of tropical moisture, originating from the western Caribbean Sea, spread northward along the east coast of the United States. As this moisture interacted with a stationary boundary across Virginia, widespread moderate to heavy rain fell across the Mid-Atlantic September 27.  Rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches caused minor isolated flash flooding in Montgomery County, Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash Flooding was reported at the intersection of Route 28 and Avery Road. There was also additional flooding along Rock Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system moved northward up the eastern seaboard along a north-to-south oriented stationary front. Very moist low level atmosphere and 70 knot southeasterly winds combined with modest instability to produce damaging winds in the strongest thunderstorms that developed.  An isolated tornado also touched down in Anne Arundel County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage consisted of uprooted and snapped trees, most along Milburn Circle. One tree fell on a vehicle; another crushed a shed. There was also minor siding and shingle damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure and strong southerly winds allowed water to pool in the Chesapeake Bay and be pushed onshore. Water was also pushed upstream the tidal Potomac River. Coastal flooding was observed along the lower tidal Potomac River and across the Chespeake Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed due to tidal inundation.  Shirley Blvd. was closed at Port Tobacco Lakes.  Saunders Marine Place was closed in Charleston Creek.  Cobb Island Road was closed on Cobb island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low level jet stream allowed strong winds to mix down to the surface in the morning, causing damage in St. Mary's County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three trees down in Leonardtown, one of which fell onto a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low pressure sitting over the eastern Great Lakes brought prolonged cold northwest flow across the region producing intense lake effect snow bands into northwest Pennsylvania and the higher ridges of western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Storm total snowfall ranged from 6 to 10 inches with isolated 12 inch reports across northwest Pennsylvania and extended south into Butler county. Amounts in the ridges of western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland ranged from 12 to 18 inches.

A low pressure system moved east across the upper Ohio Valley bringing mixed precipitation followed by cold northwest flow. Lake effect snow bands produced heavy snow in Mercer county Pennsylvania, as well as the ridges in Westmoreland and Fayette counties in Pennsylvania. Heavy snow also fell in the ridges of Preston and Tucker counties of West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Storm totals of 6 to 10 inches of snow fell across portions of pennsylavania, with more than one foot of snow in portions of West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland.

An approaching cold front combined with strong winds aloft and an unseasonably mild air mass to trigger a line of showers and thunderstorms during the late morning of the 1st across the Maryland Eastern Shore. Isolated severe thunderstorms formed along this line and exacerbated the wind damage that had already occurred because of the strong winds triggered by the pressure gradient or difference between a strong high pressure system over the Canadian Maritimes and a low pressure system on the approaching cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm caused wind damage in Elkton. The thunderstorm blew over a shed and a fence and also tore down an awning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably moist air mass coupled with light winds helped form dense fog across the southern Eastern Shore around sunrise on the 15th. A weak cold front moved through the Eastern Shore later in the day on the 15th and brought slightly drier air into the region.

An unseasonably moist air mass coupled with light winds helped form dense fog across the southern Eastern Shore around sunrise on the 15th. A weak cold front moved through the Eastern Shore later in the day on the 15th and brought slightly drier air into the region.

An unseasonably moist air mass coupled with light winds helped form dense fog across the southern Eastern Shore around sunrise on the 15th. A weak cold front moved through the Eastern Shore later in the day on the 15th and brought slightly drier air into the region.

A cold front moved across the Appalachian Mountains early on the morning of December 1 into an unseasonably warm and moisture atmosphere in the Mid-Atlantic. Widespread rain and thunderstorms developed, some of which produced heavy rain and flash flooding in Allegany County. Rainfall amounts of over 2 inches were common, including an amount of 2.76 in Cumberland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several basements were flooded in Cumberland. A rain gauge nearby recorded 2.39 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first widespread measurable but overall light snow event of the winter season came at an unfortunate time during the afternoon of the 16th across the Eastern Shore. Accumulations averaged one to two inches. The recent cold weather made it easy for the snow to stick. Coupled with it coinciding with the school afternoon and start of the evening commutes led to a higher number of accidents across the Eastern Shore. ||In Cecil County, three students were injured in one of three bus accidents. A bus filled with Bohemia Manor Middle and High School Students  was rear ended by a car on Court House Point Road in Chesapeake City. Three students were treated for non-life threatening injuries at Union Hospital. In the other busing accidents, a bus carrying Perryville Middle School students on Perrylawn Drive in Perryville was rear-ended by a Jeep and another bus was rear ended by a car while taking North East High School students home on Tonys Road near Elkton. All after school activities and sports were cancelled in Cecil County. ||Representative snowfall included 1.8 inches in Easton (Talbot County), 1.2 inches in Denton (Caroline County), 1.0 inch in Queenstown (Queen Anne's County) and Milton (Kent County) and 0.4 inches in Elkton (Cecil County). The light accumulating snow was caused by a rapidly moving low pressure system that went from the Texas Red River valley on the evening of the 15th, to the Tennessee Valley at sunrise on the 16th and off the North Carolina Coast by 7 p.m. EST on the 16th. The rapid movement precluded heavier snow from occurring.

A major winter storm dropped moderate amounts of snow along the Maryland Eastern Shore as the region was near the western edge of the precipitation area. Accumulations averaged 3 to 6 inches with the highest amounts toward the Delaware state line. The storm did claim the life of an elderly woman in Talbot County who went outdoors and died of hypothermia. Intermittent light snow fell overnight on the 25th, but the storm related snow began on the 26th between 6 a.m. EST and 7 a.m. EST in Caroline and Talbot Counties, between 10 a.m. EST and 11 a.m. EST in Kent and Queen Anne's Counties and by 1 p.m. in Cecil County. The snow ended during the early morning on the 27th before sunrise. The winter storm occurring on a Sunday (26th) cut down on the number of traffic accidents. One of the worst reported accidents occurred near Easton (Talbot County) on U.S. Route 322 where a head-on collision resulted in multiple injuries. The winter storm forced Caroline County offices to open two hours later than normal on Monday the 27th.||Representative snowfall included 6.4 inches in Greensboro (Cecil County), 6.3 inches in Denton (Cecil County), 5.5 inches in Millington (Kent County), 4.0 inches in Conowingo (Cecil County) and America Corner (Caroline County) and 3.1 inches in Saint Michaels and Easton (Talbot County).||The low pressure system responsible for the winter weather entered the United States in southern California on December 22nd. It moved across the southern Rockies and reached the northern Gulf of Mexico on Christmas Day. It was still a relatively weak low pressure system, but as it moved across the Gulf of Mexico, it started to strengthen. At 7 p.m. EST on the 25th, the low had deepened to 1004 millibars in the eastern Gulf of Mexico. From there, the low made a turn to the northeast while the intensification process increased. At 1 a.m. EST on the 26th, it was a 1001 millibar low, just east of Georgia, at 7 a.m. EST on the 26th a 992 millibar low just east of Wilmington, North Carolina and at 1 p.m. EST on the 26th a 986 millibar low about 100 miles east of Virginia Beach, Virginia. During the next six hours as the upper level support neared,  the low's intensification rate peaked and at 7 p.m. EST on the 26th, the surface pressure fell to 972 millibars as it passed about 100 miles east of Long Beach Island, New Jersey. Overnight, the surface low was captured by the upper level low and this slowed its forward progress. The capturing phase occurred too late and the general offshore storm track prevented heavy snow bands from making it into the Eastern Shore. At 1 a.m. EST on the 27th, the 970 millibar surface low was located about 50 miles southeast of Montauk Point, New York and at 7 a.m. EST, the 962 millibar low was located just southeast of Cape Cod, Massachusetts. The surface pressure bottomed at 961 millibars at 10 a.m. EST on the 27th and from there it slowly weakened as it passed south of Nova Scotia. The surface pressure of this winter storm dropped 31 millibars from 1 a.m. EST on the 26th to 1 a.m. EST on the 27th, well exceeding the meteorological definition of a bombing low pressure system (24 millibar drop in 24 hours).

Strong winds occurred on the 27th in the wake of the departing winter storm. Peak wind gusts were near 50 mph and knocked down some weak tree limbs and power lines. The largest effect though was the considerable blowing and drifting of the snow the wind caused. This hampered plowing and clean-up operations after the snow ended.||The strong winds were caused by the pressure gradient (difference) between the winter storm low pressure system and a strong high pressure building east from the central part of the United States. Winds eased as the low moved farther away from the Middle Atlantic States as the day progressed on the 27th as well as it stopped intensifying after the morning of the 27th.  The low pressure system's surface pressure bottomed at 961 millibars at 10 a.m. EST on the 27th as it was passing just east of Cape Cod, Massachusetts.

Low pressure moving north just off the Mid Atlantic Coast produced between four and fifteen inches of snow across the Lower Maryland Eastern Shore from Saturday evening, December 25th, into early Monday morning December 27th. Also, the storm produced near blizzard conditions over portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally between nine and fourteen inches across the county. Ocean City reported 13.5 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally between nine and fifteen inches across the county. Pocomoke City reported 15.0 inches of snow. Snow Hill reported 13.0 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first widespread measurable but overall light snow event of the winter season came at an unfortunate time during the afternoon of the 16th across the Eastern Shore. Accumulations averaged one to two inches. The recent cold weather made it easy for the snow to stick. Coupled with it coinciding with the school afternoon and start of the evening commutes led to a higher number of accidents across the Eastern Shore. ||In Cecil County, three students were injured in one of three bus accidents. A bus filled with Bohemia Manor Middle and High School Students  was rear ended by a car on Court House Point Road in Chesapeake City. Three students were treated for non-life threatening injuries at Union Hospital. In the other busing accidents, a bus carrying Perryville Middle School students on Perrylawn Drive in Perryville was rear-ended by a Jeep and another bus was rear ended by a car while taking North East High School students home on Tonys Road near Elkton. All after school activities and sports were cancelled in Cecil County. ||Representative snowfall included 1.8 inches in Easton (Talbot County), 1.2 inches in Denton (Caroline County), 1.0 inch in Queenstown (Queen Anne's County) and Milton (Kent County) and 0.4 inches in Elkton (Cecil County). The light accumulating snow was caused by a rapidly moving low pressure system that went from the Texas Red River valley on the evening of the 15th, to the Tennessee Valley at sunrise on the 16th and off the North Carolina Coast by 7 p.m. EST on the 16th. The rapid movement precluded heavier snow from occurring.

Low pressure cut up through the great lakes during the early morning hours of the 12th. Warm and moist air overrode the surface cold air in place causing precipitation to occur. There was enough cold air at the surface for pockets of freezing rain across portions of western Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice from freezing rain accumulated up to twelve hundredths of an inch at Eckhart Mines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure brought a period of snow to lower southern Maryland during the afternoon of the 25th into the early morning hours of the 26th. ||Low pressure rapidly intensified as it moved up the Mid-Atlantic later coast on the 26th. Another band of snow that was associated with this system affected lower southern Maryland into the early morning hours of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals between two and three inches were reported across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals between one and two inches were reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure centered over New England wedged into the Mid-Atlantic on the 24th. A storm system developed over the central portion of the nation and the warm front associated with this system triggered wintry precipitation across western Maryland. High pressure kept enough low-level cold air in place for freezing rain across portions of western Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow, sleet, and freezing rain fell across western Allegany County on the 24th into the 25th. Snow and sleet amounts were light while ice accumulations from freezing rain were estimated to be around one tenth of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed through the region in a zonal flow causing precipitation to develop on the 16th. There was plenty of cold air in place ahead of this system causing precipitation to fall in the form of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was estimated around two inches across eastern Allegany County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving across the Mid Atlantic region and off the coast produced between one and three inches of snow across portions of the Lower Maryland Eastern Shore during Thursday, December 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally between one inch and three inches across the county. |Toddville reported 1.8 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through the Ohio Valley before redeveloping off the Mid-Atlantic Coast. High pressure to the north kept just enough cold air for precipitation that was associated with this low to fall in the from of snow and ice. The highest snow and ice totals were across the western portion of Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall around two inches was reported across western Allegany County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall around two inches was reported across central and eastern Allegany County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through the Ohio Valley before redeveloping off the Mid-Atlantic Coast. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trace of ice was reported near Damascus. However, most of the precipitation fell as rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds occurred on the 27th in the wake of the departing winter storm. Peak wind gusts were near 50 mph and knocked down some weak tree limbs and power lines. The largest effect though was the considerable blowing and drifting of the snow the wind caused. This hampered plowing and clean-up operations after the snow ended.||The strong winds were caused by the pressure gradient (difference) between the winter storm low pressure system and a strong high pressure building east from the central part of the United States. Winds eased as the low moved farther away from the Middle Atlantic States as the day progressed on the 27th as well as it stopped intensifying after the morning of the 27th.  The low pressure system's surface pressure bottomed at 961 millibars at 10 a.m. EST on the 27th as it was passing just east of Cape Cod, Massachusetts.

A backdoor cold front stalled out over the region on the 12th while and upper-level disturbance passed through the area. A hot and humid air mass ahead of the cold front produced moderate amounts of instability. Some thunderstorms that developed were able to produce damaging wind and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell into the Garden Apartments. The tree was eighty feet tall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passed through the region late during the early morning morning hours of the 17th. A strong southerly flow ahead of the cold front transported plenty of moisture into the area allowing for limited instability to develop. Strong forcing associated with the frontal passage combined with limited instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Thunderstorms were able to mix down very strong winds aloft causing damaging winds across some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were down near Morgan State University. The path of tree damage continued to the northeast into southern Baltimore County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The pressure gradient or difference between a strong high pressure system over the Canadian Maritimes and a low pressure system on the approaching cold front produced strong south winds across the Eastern Shore during the first half of the day of December 1st. Wind gusts were enhanced around a line of showers and isolated thunderstorms that preceded and accompanied the cold frontal passage during the late morning. Peak wind gusts average around 45 mph and knocked down weak tree limbs and wires and caused isolated power outages. Peak wind gusts included 48 mph at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport, 47 mph in Stevensville (Queen Anne's County), 44 mph in Salisbury (Wicomico County) and 41 mph at Tolchester Beach (Kent County).  The up the Chesapeake Bay southerly flow and then westerly flow during the early afternoon caused minor tidal flooding around high tide during the afternoon of the 1st. The tide reached 3.72 feet above mean lower low water at Tolchester Beach (Kent County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 3.5 feet above mean lower low water.

The continuation of near normal precipitation and the drop in water demand with the end of the growing season permitted the Maryland Department of the Environment to cancel all drought watches for the Maryland Eastern Shore. ||The drought and summer heat took its toll on Eastern Shore farmers and the United States Secretary of Agriculture Thomas Vilsack declared all counties in the Eastern Shore natural disaster areas. The declaration permitted affected farmers, ranchers and other agricultural producers eligible to apply for low interest emergency loans from the Farm Service Agency. Nearly all Christmas tree growers in Maryland lost some trees to drought during the summer. The trees most affected were younger than three years old.

A squall line of showers and thunderstorms that preceded a cold front produced wind damage during the early morning on the 17th across the Maryland Eastern Shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a telephone pole at the intersection of Maryland State Route 514 and Mary Morris Road in Chestertown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of showers and thunderstorms that preceded a cold front produced wind damage during the early morning on the 17th across the Maryland Eastern Shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous trees from St. Michaels east through Easton. The greatest concentration of wind damage occurred southwest of Easton. Trees were knocked down near Peachblossom Creek on Old Country Road. A sailboat in one garage was damaged by a downed tree. On Bailey's Neck Road, dozens of trees were knocked down. One downed tree shattered the roof of a garage. A couple of homes in the area suffered siding damage and had outdoor furniture damaged.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees and wires in Oxford.

Another in a series of low pressure areas moved across the Ohio valley with cold northwest developing behind it. Bands of heavier snow fell across Mercer and Fayette counties in Pennsylvania, Garrett county Maryland, and the ridges of northern West Virginia. Snowfall totals ranged from 8 to 14 inches in these areas, with lesser amounts across the remainder of western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.

Strong southerly winds affected the Eastern Shore during the morning of the 25th.  Peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 50 mph, with the strongest winds in the southern part of the Eastern Shore. The strong winds caused isolated power outages as the combination of the rain and wind helped knock down weak trees, tree limbs and power lines. In Queen Anne's County, downed trees damaged two homes in Centreville. Across the Eastern Shore about 2,300 homes and businesses lost power. |  |Peak winds included 55 mph in Salisbury (Wicomico County), 48 mph in Easton (Talbot County), 47 mph in Stevensville (Queen Anne's County) and 43 mph in Tolchester Beach (Kent County). A wind gust of 58 mph was measured across Chesapeake Bay in Annapolis.| |The strong winds were caused by a tight surface pressure gradient (difference) between a low pressure system that was moving north just to the east of the central Appalachians and a high pressure system over the western Atlantic Ocean. The strong southerly flow started to mix down to the surface after the low pressure system���s warm front moved north of the state. The winds subsided during the afternoon as the low pressure system���s cold front approached the area and the area of strong winds between 3000 feet and 5000 feet moved away from the Eastern Shore.

Heavy rain and snow melt on the 25th caused flooding along the Susquehanna River on the 26th and 27th. The strong south winds up Chesapeake Bay also caused tidal flooding during the afternoon of the 25th in Queen Anne's and Kent Counties. ||The afternoon high tide caused flooding in the Kent Narrows area of Queen Anne's County. Flooding occurred along Maryland State Route 18 and Wharf Road in Chester. At high tide both directions of Maryland State Route 18 near Dundee Avenue was closed. The same roadway was also closed near Love Point. The high tide reached 4.51 feet above mean lower low water at 206 p.m. EST on the 25th at Tolchester Beach (Kent County). Moderate tidal flooding starts at 4.50 feet above mean lower low water. The tide was above the level for minor tidal flooding (3.5 feet above mean lower low water) from 1048 a.m. EST through 618 p.m. EST on the 25th.||The heavy rain and strong south winds were caused by a strong south flow from the Gulf of Mexico. A low pressure system formed over the Gulf Coast states on the morning of the 24th and moved just east of the Appalachians and passed through Maryland and Pennsylvania during the morning of the 25th. The strong southerly flow and rain ended after its cold front moved through the Eastern Shore during the early afternoon.

The strong southerly flow and rain ended after its cold front moved through the Eastern Shore during the early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Susquehanna River at the Conowingo Dam was above its 23.5 foot flood stage from 725 a.m. EST through 1109 p.m. EST on the 27th. It crested at 24.37 feet at 915 p.m. EST.  While the flooding was caused by the heavy rain and snow melt through the Susquehanna Basin, event precipitation totals on the 24th and 25th in Cecil County included 1.55 inches in Elkton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds affected the Eastern Shore during the morning of the 25th.  Peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 50 mph, with the strongest winds in the southern part of the Eastern Shore. The strong winds caused isolated power outages as the combination of the rain and wind helped knock down weak trees, tree limbs and power lines. In Queen Anne's County, downed trees damaged two homes in Centreville. Across the Eastern Shore about 2,300 homes and businesses lost power. |  |Peak winds included 55 mph in Salisbury (Wicomico County), 48 mph in Easton (Talbot County), 47 mph in Stevensville (Queen Anne's County) and 43 mph in Tolchester Beach (Kent County). A wind gust of 58 mph was measured across Chesapeake Bay in Annapolis.| |The strong winds were caused by a tight surface pressure gradient (difference) between a low pressure system that was moving north just to the east of the central Appalachians and a high pressure system over the western Atlantic Ocean. The strong southerly flow started to mix down to the surface after the low pressure system���s warm front moved north of the state. The winds subsided during the afternoon as the low pressure system���s cold front approached the area and the area of strong winds between 3000 feet and 5000 feet moved away from the Eastern Shore.

Double barrel low pressure systems brought a light accumulating snow to the Eastern Shore on the evening and overnight on the 2nd. Snowfall averaged 2 to 4 inches. Precipitation started during the early evening of the 2nd and initially began as rain in parts of Caroline and Talbot Counties. The rain changed to snow before Midnight EST and the snow ended by 5 a.m. EST on the 3rd. Since the snow held off until after the evening commute was done and stopped before the morning commute started, the number of reported accidents was low.||Representative snowfall included 3.5 inches in Colora (Cecil County) and Stevensville (Queen Anne's County), 3.2 inches in Millington (Kent County), 2.5 inches in Elkton (Cecil County), 2.4 inches in Greensboro and Denton (Caroline County), 2.0 inches in Easton (Talbot County), 1.7 inches in Trappe (Talbot County) and 1.4 inches in Saint Michaels (Talbot County).| |The snow was caused by a pair of low pressure systems: one that was moving through the southern Great Lakes and another that was forming off the Georgia coast on the morning of the 2nd. The latter low pressure system intensified as it moved northeast off the southeastern coast overnight on the 2nd while the former dissipated.

A clipper type low pressure system caused light snow to fall for around six hours across the Eastern Shore during the first part of the day on the 8th. Accumulations averaged around 2 inches and fell mainly between Midnight EST and 6 a.m. EST on the 8th. The early arrival during the day and recent cold weather made untreated roadways slippery for the morning commute. Actual snowfall included 2.3 inches in Denton (Caroline County) and Millington (Kent County), 2.2 inches in Easton (Talbot County), 1.6 inches in Colora (Cecil County) and 1.5 inches in Stevensville (Queen Anne's County).  ||The clipper low pressure system moved from Illinois on the morning of the 7th, to Ohio during the early evening on the 7th, to the Poconos at 7 a.m. EST on the 8th and well off the New England Coast by 7 p.m. EST that evening. The snow ended shortly after the clipper's occluded and cold fronts moved through the Eastern Shore.

A strong cold front passed through the region late during the early morning morning hours of the 17th. A strong southerly flow ahead of the cold front transported plenty of moisture into the area allowing for limited instability to develop. Strong forcing associated with the frontal passage combined with limited instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Thunderstorms were able to mix down very strong winds aloft causing damaging winds across some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were down from thunderstorm wind gusts from the city of Baltimore northeast to Gunpowder Falls State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent area of low pressure tracked through the Midwest on the 9th. As the low approached the Mid-Atlantic coast, this system phased with energy in the southern branch of the jet stream to cause strong low pressure to develop just off the Delmarva peninsula. The low continued to rapidly strengthen as it moved off to our northeast on the 10th. ||The system spread snow across Maryland that began on the 9th and lasted into the 10th. Major snow accumulations were reported with this storm, especially across the northeastern portion of Maryland. As the low pressure rapidly intensified, strong winds caused blowing and drifting snow that led to blizzard conditions across portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility was below one quarter mile due to blowing and drifting snow. The low visibility combined with wind gusts over 35 mph to produce blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent area of low pressure strengthened over the central portion of the nation on the fifth of February. The storm system slowly moved through the Mid-Atlantic during the night of the 5th before redeveloping off the Mid-Atlantic coast on 6th. The storm system finally moved away from the area on the night of the 6th. ||Strong high pressure continued to pump in plenty of cold air across the region for the entire event. Due to the slow movement of the storm, there was a prolonged period of precipitation. The storm system ushered in copious amounts of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean. The deep moisture combined with the forcing from the storm system to bring a period of heavy precipitation to the area on the night of the 5th through the daytime hours on the 6th. Precipitation finally ended during the evening hours of the 6th as the storm system moved away. Most of the precipitation fell in the form of snow due to the cold air that was already in place.  ||Major snow accumulations were reported throughout the state of Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 19.0 inches near Hillville. Snow totaled up to 17.0 near Sotterley and Hollywood.||Power outages were reported throughout the county due to the weight of the snow on trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm dropped 20 to 30 inches of snow across the Maryland Eastern Shore from the afternoon of the 5th into the afternoon of the 6th. Snow began falling along Chesapeake Bay around Noon EST on the 5th and spread east throughout the Eastern Shore by the middle of the afternoon. The snow fell at its heaviest during the first half of the day on the 6th. The snow ended from north to south during the afternoon of the 6th. Many businesses and stores were closed on the 6th. Many states of emergencies were declared on both the township and county level. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in Cecil County while lighter snow fell across the rest of the Eastern Shore from from the late evening of the 24th into the early afternoon of the 26th. Snowfall averaged three to five inches in Cecil County and around one inch in Kent and Queen Anne's Counties. Most of the snow melted as it fell in Caroline and Talbot Counties. Precipitation initially started as rain during the afternoon of the 24th in the southern Eastern Shore and spread north during the evening and overnight of the 24th. As the intensity increased, the rain changed to snow in Talbot and Caroline Counties late in the evening on the 24th. This changeover spread north overnight.|The snow ended during the morning of the 25th, but started again during the evening of the 25th in Cecil County and overnight across the rest of the Eastern Shore. The snow ended in all places during the early afternoon of the 26th. Aside from Cecil County, the snow never fell heavily over the rest of the Eastern Shore and the number of reported accidents were relatively low. Winds increased before the heavier snow returned and this cut down on the number of power outages. The strongest winds occurred between 6 p.m. EST on the 25th and 6 p.m. EST on the 26th.||Representative snowfall included 4.5 inches in Colora (Cecil County). Peak wind gusts included 40 mph in Easton (Talbot County).||The winter storm was caused by a low pressure system that formed east of Florida on the 24th. At 7 a.m. EST on the 25th, it (992 millibar low pressure system) was well east of the North Carolina coast and was intensifying rapidly as it was moving north. At 1 p.m. EST on the 25th, it was a 987 millibar low well east of the Delmarva Peninsula. At 7 p.m. EST on the 25th, it was a 978 millibar low just east of Long Island. At 10 p.m. EST on the 25th, it deepened to 972 millibars and was located along the Rhode Island and Massachusetts border. From there, a rare occurrence happened as the low pressure system retrograded or move westward. At 1 a.m. EST on the 26th, the 972 millibar low was in Connecticut; at 7 a.m. EST it weakened to 979 millibars and was over New York City. It spent the rest of the daylight hours weakening over the New York City area and then drifted slowly north that night. The   retrogression process caused the heavier snow to back into Cecil County on the 26th.

Strong low pressure moving off the northern Mid Atlantic Coast produced blizzard conditions across the Lower Maryland Eastern Shore from Tuesday afternoon, February 9th, into Wednesday evening February 10th. Snow, heavy at times, occurred with northwest winds 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph, resulting in poor visibilities and even whiteout conditions. Total snow amounts ranged from two inches to as much as fifteen inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally between five and eight inches across the county. Princess Anne reported 8.0 inches of snow. Deal Island reported 6.5 inches of snow. Snow, heavy at times, occurred with northwest winds 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph, resulting in poor visibilities and even whiteout conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time within a week a major winter storm, this one with blizzard conditions at times,  affected the Maryland Eastern Shore. Blizzard conditions occurred at times during the late morning and the first half of the afternoon on the 10th. Snowfall averaged around one foot with the highest amounts in Kent and Cecil Counties. Ice accretions were less than one-quarter of an inch.||Across the lower Eastern Shore, precipitation started briefly as rain or sleet during the late afternoon on the 9th. As the precipitation intensity increased, the rain and sleet changed to snow by the middle of the evening. As warmer air moved in aloft, the snow changed back to sleet and freezing rain during the second half of the evening. Surface temperatures responded more slowly and the sleet and freezing rain changed over to rain just before midnight EST on the 10th. Some sleet mixed in from time to time. As the low pressure system moved northeast of the state, the rain changed back to snow around 9 a.m. EST on the 10th and fell heavy at times from mid morning into the early afternoon. Winds also increased and blizzard conditions occurred during the late morning and early afternoon of the 10th. The snow ended in the evening on the 10th. In the upper or northern Eastern Shore, precipitation started as snow during the late afternoon of the 9th and fell heavy at times. As warmer air moved in aloft there too, the snow changed over to sleet and freezing rain just after midnight EST on the 10th. Some surface temperatures rose above freezing and the precipitation fell as plain rain into the middle of the morning (around 9 a.m. EST) when it changed back to snow and fell heavy at times into the early afternoon. Blizzard conditions developed during the late morning and early afternoon of the 10th. The snow ended in the evening on the 10th.||The heavy snow clinged to the trees and then the increase in winds caused power outages, especially in Cecil County. About 13,600 homes and businesses lost power in Cecil County. The weight of the heavy snow also caused a canopy collapse at Hack's Point in Bohemia Bay (Cecil County) and badly damaged thirty-eight boats.  Many city, federal, social and county offices as well as courthouses were closed on the 10th. Municipal meetings were canceled. Schools were closed on the 10th and 11th, some even on the 12th. A limited number of businesses were opened on the 10th. Many school districts ran out of snow days and municipal snow removal budgets were exceeded. Trash collections were postponed. In addition, the weight of the two snows caused about 50 chicken houses to collapse on the Delmarva Peninsula.    ||Representative snowfall included 16.5 inches in Millington (Kent County), 16.3 inches in Greensboro (Caroline County), 12.5 inches in Stevensville (Queen Anne's County), 12.0 inches in Easton (Talbot County), 11.8 inches in Port Herman (Cecil County), 9.0 inches in Saint Michaels (Talbot County), 8.0 inches in Fairlee (Kent County), 7.0 inches at Denton (Caroline County) and 6.9 inches in St. Michaels (Talbot County).  Peak wind gusts included 55 mph at Royal Oak (Talbot County), 43 mph in Easton (Talbot County) and 40 mph at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport. ||The low pressure system that caused the latest winter storm and blizzard emerged from the Big Bend of Texas on the morning of the 8th. It moved northeast and reached the Tennessee Valley on the morning of the 9th. At 7 p.m. EST on the 9th, it was located near Charleston, South Carolina. It then moved northnortheast and was near Norfolk, Virginia at 1 a.m. EST on the 10th, Georgetown, Delaware at 7 a.m. EST on the 10th, Atlantic City, New Jersey at 10 a.m. EST on the 10th and just east of Seaside Heights, New Jersey at 4 p.m. EST on the 10th. The low pressure system then drifted slowly east the rest of the afternoon into the overnight of the 10th. The pass of the low pressure system into the state permitted warmer air aloft and at the surface to make it into the Eastern Shore from the late evening of the 9th into the first half of the day on the 10th, before the heavier snow and then blizzard conditions occurred.

There were fender bender accidents on the 5th, but because this event ended on a Saturday (the 6th), the total number of accidents was relatively lower.  ||Representative snowfall included 30.0 inches in Galena and Millington (Kent County), 29.5 inches in Royal Oak (Talbot County), 28.0 inches in Chester and Stevensville (Queen Anne's County), 24.0 inches in Saint Michaels (Talbot County), 23.5 in Easton (Talbot County), 23.0 inches in Denton (Caroline County), 21.0 inches in Elkton (Cecil County) and 18.0 inches in Fair Hills (Cecil County).||The winter storm was caused by a low pressure system that moved west to east across northern Mexico and emerged in the Gulf of Mexico near Brownsville, Texas on the morning of the 4th. The low pressure system moved east and was located near New Orleans, Louisiana at 7 p.m. EST on the 4th and Mobile, Alabama at 7 a.m. EST on the 5th. The low pressure system then started to move northeast and was near Wilmington, North Carolina at 7 p.m. EST on the 5th; Elizabeth City, North Carolina at 1 a.m. EST on the 6th; the Virginia coastal waters at 7 a.m. EST on the 6th and then drifted slowly east off the lower Delmarva Peninsula the rest of the day on the 6th.  The storm track was perfect for heavy snow for the Eastern Shore.

A large and slow moving area of low pressure developed across the Ohio valley on the 25th and intensified as it moved east to the Mid-Atlantic states. Heavy snow fell in the ridges of Garrett county Maryland, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania with storm totals of 12 to 36 inches. Highest reported storm totals were 34 inches at Red House in Garrett county, MD and 33 inches in Farmington in Fayette county, PA. Northwest winds gusting at times over 30 MPH also caused severe blowing and drifting of snow with drifts of 8 to 10 feet reported. Elsewhere over western Pennsylvania, eastern Ohio and northern West Virginia a prolonged snow event of 36 to 48 hours brought storm totals of 6 to 10 inches with minor blowing and drufting of snow.

A potent area of low pressure strengthened over the central portion of the nation on the fifth of February. The storm system slowly moved through the Mid-Atlantic during the night of the 5th before redeveloping off the Mid-Atlantic coast on 6th. The storm system finally moved away from the area on the night of the 6th. ||Strong high pressure continued to pump in plenty of cold air across the region for the entire event. Due to the slow movement of the storm, there was a prolonged period of precipitation. The storm system ushered in copious amounts of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean. The deep moisture combined with the forcing from the storm system to bring a period of heavy precipitation to the area on the night of the 5th through the daytime hours on the 6th. Precipitation finally ended during the evening hours of the 6th as the storm system moved away. Most of the precipitation fell in the form of snow due to the cold air that was already in place.  ||Major snow accumulations were reported throughout the state of Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts over 35 mph were reported across portions of southern Baltimore County. The wind combined with visibility below one quarter of a mile due to snow and blowing snow to cause blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving off the coastal Carolinas produced between six and twenty inches of snow across the Lower Maryland Eastern Shore from Friday afternoon, February 5th, through Saturday afternoon February 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally between ten and twenty inches across the county. Hurlock reported 20.0 inches of snow. Toddville reported 18.0 inches of snow. Cambridge reported 16.0 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent area of low pressure tracked through the Midwest on the 9th. As the low approached the Mid-Atlantic coast, this system phased with energy in the southern branch of the jet stream to cause strong low pressure to develop just off the Delmarva peninsula. The low continued to rapidly strengthen as it moved off to our northeast on the 10th. ||The system spread snow across Maryland that began on the 9th and lasted into the 10th. Major snow accumulations were reported with this storm, especially across the northeastern portion of Maryland. As the low pressure rapidly intensified, strong winds caused blowing and drifting snow that led to blizzard conditions across portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 8.5 inches at Eckhart Mines and 7.8 inches near Frostburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the Eastern Shore from the morning of the 30th into the early morning of the 31st. Snowfall averaged 4 to 10 inches with the highest amounts in the southern part of the Eastern Shore. Snow spread from south to north from 9 a.m. EST to Noon EST during the morning of the 30th. It fell at its heaviest during the afternoon and evening and ended from north to south between Midnight EST and 4 a.m. EST on the 31st. Many accidents were reported in the Central Eastern Shore between 11 a.m. EST and 3 p.m. EST on the 30th. Schools in Queen Annes County had a 90 minute delayed opening on Monday February 1st.||Representative snowfall included 10.5 inches in Royal Oak (Talbot County), 8.3 inches in Stevensville (Queen Anne���s County), 8.0 inches in Millington (Kent County), 7.5 inches in Greensboro (Caroline County) and Rock Hall (Kent County), 7.0 inches in Chestertown (Kent County), Easton (Talbot County) and Trappe (Talbot County), 6.0 inches in Queenstown (Queen Anne���s County), 5.5 inches in Colora (Cecil County) and 4.0 inches in Elkton (Cecil County). ||The heavy snow was caused by a low pressure system that moved along the southern Gulf Coast states from the morning of the 29th into the morning of the 30th. The low reformed over the Carolina coastal waters later in the day on the 30th and moved quickly northeast that evening and overnight.

For the second time within a week a major winter storm, this one with blizzard conditions at times,  affected the Maryland Eastern Shore. Blizzard conditions occurred at times during the late morning and the first half of the afternoon on the 10th. Snowfall averaged around one foot with the highest amounts in Kent and Cecil Counties. Ice accretions were less than one-quarter of an inch.||Across the lower Eastern Shore, precipitation started briefly as rain or sleet during the late afternoon on the 9th. As the precipitation intensity increased, the rain and sleet changed to snow by the middle of the evening. As warmer air moved in aloft, the snow changed back to sleet and freezing rain during the second half of the evening. Surface temperatures responded more slowly and the sleet and freezing rain changed over to rain just before midnight EST on the 10th. Some sleet mixed in from time to time. As the low pressure system moved northeast of the state, the rain changed back to snow around 9 a.m. EST on the 10th and fell heavy at times from mid morning into the early afternoon. Winds also increased and blizzard conditions occurred during the late morning and early afternoon of the 10th. The snow ended in the evening on the 10th. In the upper or northern Eastern Shore, precipitation started as snow during the late afternoon of the 9th and fell heavy at times. As warmer air moved in aloft there too, the snow changed over to sleet and freezing rain just after midnight EST on the 10th. Some surface temperatures rose above freezing and the precipitation fell as plain rain into the middle of the morning (around 9 a.m. EST) when it changed back to snow and fell heavy at times into the early afternoon. Blizzard conditions developed during the late morning and early afternoon of the 10th. The snow ended in the evening on the 10th.||The heavy snow clinged to the trees and then the increase in winds caused power outages, especially in Cecil County. About 13,600 homes and businesses lost power in Cecil County. The weight of the heavy snow also caused a canopy collapse at Hack's Point in Bohemia Bay (Cecil County) and badly damaged thirty-eight boats.  Many city, federal, social and county offices as well as courthouses were closed on the 10th. Municipal meetings were canceled. Schools were closed on the 10th and 11th, some even on the 12th. A limited number of businesses were opened on the 10th. Many school districts ran out of snow days and municipal snow removal budgets were exceeded. Trash collections were postponed. In addition, the weight of the two snows caused about 50 chicken houses to collapse on the Delmarva Peninsula.    ||Representative snowfall included 16.5 inches in Millington (Kent County), 16.3 inches in Greensboro (Caroline County), 12.5 inches in Stevensville (Queen Anne's County), 12.0 inches in Easton (Talbot County), 11.8 inches in Port Herman (Cecil County), 9.0 inches in Saint Michaels (Talbot County), 8.0 inches in Fairlee (Kent County), 7.0 inches at Denton (Caroline County) and 6.9 inches in St. Michaels (Talbot County).  Peak wind gusts included 55 mph at Royal Oak (Talbot County), 43 mph in Easton (Talbot County) and 40 mph at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport. ||The low pressure system that caused the latest winter storm and blizzard emerged from the Big Bend of Texas on the morning of the 8th. It moved northeast and reached the Tennessee Valley on the morning of the 9th. At 7 p.m. EST on the 9th, it was located near Charleston, South Carolina. It then moved northnortheast and was near Norfolk, Virginia at 1 a.m. EST on the 10th, Georgetown, Delaware at 7 a.m. EST on the 10th, Atlantic City, New Jersey at 10 a.m. EST on the 10th and just east of Seaside Heights, New Jersey at 4 p.m. EST on the 10th. The low pressure system then drifted slowly east the rest of the afternoon into the overnight of the 10th. The pass of the low pressure system into the state permitted warmer air aloft and at the surface to make it into the Eastern Shore from the late evening of the 9th into the first half of the day on the 10th, before the heavier snow and then blizzard conditions occurred.

In the upper or northern Eastern Shore, precipitation started as snow during the late afternoon of the 9th and fell heavy at times. As warmer air moved in aloft there too, the snow changed over to sleet and freezing rain just after midnight EST on the 10th. Some surface temperatures rose above freezing and the precipitation fell as plain rain into the middle of the morning (around 9 a.m. EST) when it changed back to snow and fell heavy at times into the early afternoon. Blizzard conditions developed during the late morning and early afternoon of the 10th. The snow ended in the evening on the 10th.||The heavy snow clinged to the trees and then the increase in winds caused power outages, especially in Cecil County. About 13,600 homes and businesses lost power in Cecil County. The weight of the heavy snow also caused a canopy collapse at Hack's Point in Bohemia Bay (Cecil County) and badly damaged thirty-eight boats.  Many city, federal, social and county offices as well as courthouses were closed on the 10th. Municipal meetings were canceled. Schools were closed on the 10th and 11th, some even on the 12th. A limited number of businesses were opened on the 10th. Many school districts ran out of snow days and municipal snow removal budgets were exceeded. Trash collections were postponed. In addition, the weight of the two snows caused about 50 chicken houses to collapse on the Delmarva Peninsula.    ||Representative snowfall included 16.5 inches in Millington (Kent County), 16.3 inches in Greensboro (Caroline County), 12.5 inches in Stevensville (Queen Anne's County), 12.0 inches in Easton (Talbot County), 11.8 inches in Port Herman (Cecil County), 9.0 inches in Saint Michaels (Talbot County), 8.0 inches in Fairlee (Kent County), 7.0 inches at Denton (Caroline County) and 6.9 inches in St. Michaels (Talbot County).  Peak wind gusts included 55 mph at Royal Oak (Talbot County), 43 mph in Easton (Talbot County) and 40 mph at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport. ||The low pressure system that caused the latest winter storm and blizzard emerged from the Big Bend of Texas on the morning of the 8th. It moved northeast and reached the Tennessee Valley on the morning of the 9th. At 7 p.m. EST on the 9th, it was located near Charleston, South Carolina. It then moved northnortheast and was near Norfolk, Virginia at 1 a.m. EST on the 10th, Georgetown, Delaware at 7 a.m. EST on the 10th, Atlantic City, New Jersey at 10 a.m. EST on the 10th and just east of Seaside Heights, New Jersey at 4 p.m. EST on the 10th. The low pressure system then drifted slowly east the rest of the afternoon into the overnight of the 10th. The pass of the low pressure system into the state permitted warmer air aloft and at the surface to make it into the Eastern Shore from the late evening of the 9th into the first half of the day on the 10th, before the heavier snow and then blizzard conditions occurred.

Heavy snow fell in Cecil County while lighter snow fell across the rest of the Eastern Shore from from the late evening of the 24th into the early afternoon of the 26th. Snowfall averaged three to five inches in Cecil County and around one inch in Kent and Queen Anne's Counties. Most of the snow melted as it fell in Caroline and Talbot Counties. Precipitation initially started as rain during the afternoon of the 24th in the southern Eastern Shore and spread north during the evening and overnight of the 24th. As the intensity increased, the rain changed to snow in Talbot and Caroline Counties late in the evening on the 24th. This changeover spread north overnight.|The snow ended during the morning of the 25th, but started again during the evening of the 25th in Cecil County and overnight across the rest of the Eastern Shore. The snow ended in all places during the early afternoon of the 26th. Aside from Cecil County, the snow never fell heavily over the rest of the Eastern Shore and the number of reported accidents were relatively low. Winds increased before the heavier snow returned and this cut down on the number of power outages. The strongest winds occurred between 6 p.m. EST on the 25th and 6 p.m. EST on the 26th.||Representative snowfall included 4.5 inches in Colora (Cecil County). Peak wind gusts included 40 mph in Easton (Talbot County).||The winter storm was caused by a low pressure system that formed east of Florida on the 24th. At 7 a.m. EST on the 25th, it (992 millibar low pressure system) was well east of the North Carolina coast and was intensifying rapidly as it was moving north. At 1 p.m. EST on the 25th, it was a 987 millibar low well east of the Delmarva Peninsula. At 7 p.m. EST on the 25th, it was a 978 millibar low just east of Long Island. At 10 p.m. EST on the 25th, it deepened to 972 millibars and was located along the Rhode Island and Massachusetts border. From there, a rare occurrence happened as the low pressure system retrograded or move westward. At 1 a.m. EST on the 26th, the 972 millibar low was in Connecticut; at 7 a.m. EST it weakened to 979 millibars and was over New York City. It spent the rest of the daylight hours weakening over the New York City area and then drifted slowly north that night. The   retrogression process caused the heavier snow to back into Cecil County on the 26th.

A potent area of low pressure tracked through the Midwest on the 9th. As the low approached the Mid-Atlantic coast, this system phased with energy in the southern branch of the jet stream to cause strong low pressure to develop just off the Delmarva peninsula. The low continued to rapidly strengthen as it moved off to our northeast on the 10th. ||The system spread snow across Maryland that began on the 9th and lasted into the 10th. Major snow accumulations were reported with this storm, especially across the northeastern portion of Maryland. As the low pressure rapidly intensified, strong winds caused blowing and drifting snow that led to blizzard conditions across portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were reported between 11 and 17 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm dropped 20 to 30 inches of snow across the Maryland Eastern Shore from the afternoon of the 5th into the afternoon of the 6th. Snow began falling along Chesapeake Bay around Noon EST on the 5th and spread east throughout the Eastern Shore by the middle of the afternoon. The snow fell at its heaviest during the first half of the day on the 6th. The snow ended from north to south during the afternoon of the 6th. Many businesses and stores were closed on the 6th. Many states of emergencies were declared on both the township and county level. There were fender bender accidents on the 5th, but because this event ended on a Saturday (the 6th), the total number of accidents was relatively lower.  ||Representative snowfall included 30.0 inches in Galena and Millington (Kent County), 29.5 inches in Royal Oak (Talbot County), 28.0 inches in Chester and Stevensville (Queen Anne's County), 24.0 inches in Saint Michaels (Talbot County), 23.5 in Easton (Talbot County), 23.0 inches in Denton (Caroline County), 21.0 inches in Elkton (Cecil County) and 18.0 inches in Fair Hills (Cecil County).||The winter storm was caused by a low pressure system that moved west to east across northern Mexico and emerged in the Gulf of Mexico near Brownsville, Texas on the morning of the 4th. The low pressure system moved east and was located near New Orleans, Louisiana at 7 p.m. EST on the 4th and Mobile, Alabama at 7 a.m. EST on the 5th. The low pressure system then started to move northeast and was near Wilmington, North Carolina at 7 p.m. EST on the 5th; Elizabeth City, North Carolina at 1 a.m. EST on the 6th; the Virginia coastal waters at 7 a.m. EST on the 6th and then drifted slowly east off the lower Delmarva Peninsula the rest of the day on the 6th.  The storm track was perfect for heavy snow for the Eastern Shore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low passed through Maryland on the 15th triggering snow showers. Low pressure intensified as it slowly moved up the New England coast on the 16th and 17th before eventually moving into Canada on the 18th. A northwest flow around the low triggered a prolonged period of upslope snow showers for locations along and west of the Allegheny Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 7.0 inches in Frostburg and 2.0 inches near Eckhart Mines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low presure moved east across the Ohio Valley the afternoon of the 15th spreading snow across much of eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Snow continued in northwest flow behind the storm into the 18th with storm totals in the ridges of northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland from 12 to 18 inches. Some snow amounts across eastern Ohio were from 6 to 9 inches with a general storm total of 4 to 7 inches across the remainder of western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front over the Delmarva Peninsula helped trigger strong to locally severe thunderstorms with hail during the evening of the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell in Galena.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent area of low pressure strengthened over the central portion of the nation on the fifth of February. The storm system slowly moved through the Mid-Atlantic during the night of the 5th before redeveloping off the Mid-Atlantic coast on 6th. The storm system finally moved away from the area on the night of the 6th. ||Strong high pressure continued to pump in plenty of cold air across the region for the entire event. Due to the slow movement of the storm, there was a prolonged period of precipitation. The storm system ushered in copious amounts of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean. The deep moisture combined with the forcing from the storm system to bring a period of heavy precipitation to the area on the night of the 5th through the daytime hours on the 6th. Precipitation finally ended during the evening hours of the 6th as the storm system moved away. Most of the precipitation fell in the form of snow due to the cold air that was already in place.  ||Major snow accumulations were reported throughout the state of Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 29.5 inches about two miles northeast of Jefferson. Another report of 29.0 inches was received near Frederick. Snowfall amounts between 24 and 27 inches were reported across the rest of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure moving north from the Gulf of Mexico on February 5th  combined with low pressure across the Ohio Valley and intensified over the Mid-Atlantic states. This produced an historic snow storm across western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland, and eastern Ohio from the afternoon of February 5th to the afternoon of February 6th. Over 2 feet of snow fell across portions of Garrett county Maryland, and in Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia, with locally 3 feet in some locations. Storm totals ranged from 37 inches in Friendsville, MD to 22 inches in Terra Alta, WV. Across Pennsylvania, 18 to 24 inches of snow fell roughly between an area from SR 422 and I-70, with 21.1 inches of snow falling in Pittsburgh. This was the 4th largest snowfall on record in Pittsburgh. Over 2 feet of snow fell in the ridges of Westmoreland and Fayette counties in Pennsylvania. Along and north of I-80 in Pennsylvania 6 to 18 inches of snow fell. South of I-70 in Pennsylvania, eastern Ohio and northern West Virginia amounts ranged from 10 to 20 inches. North of I-70, in eastern Ohio, snow accumulated 12 to 18 inches. The snow was wet and heavy in Pittsburgh and areas south, bringing down trees and power lines with around 200,000 people without power at some point after the storm. Roads were not passable for 2 to 3 days in some locations, and power was not restored to some homes until 3 days after the storm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frostburg received 36.0 inches of snow and Eckhart Mines had a report of 32.0 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Four days of rain, heaviest on the 13th, culminated with poor drainage, field and some river flooding in northeast Maryland. Storm totals averaged around 3 inches. Periods of rain started during the morning of the 12th and fell at the heaviest during the first half of the day on the 13th. Periods of lighter rain persisted into the 14th and 15th. The area avoided the heaviest rain that fell with the system in New Jersey. ||The heavy rain was caused by a slow moving low pressure system that had a tremendous fetch of moist air from the Atlantic Ocean. The low pressure system moved from the South Carolina coast on the morning of the 12th, into Virginia on the morning of the 13th, to Delaware on the morning of the 14th and then drifted slowly east over the next two days as it was captured by an upper level system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Elk Creek flooded and forced the closure of Delaware Avenue at Meadow Park in Elkton. Minor flooding along the Octoraro Creek affected Port Deposit. One car was stuck in high water. The precipitation event total in Elkton was 3.30 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms associated with low pressure and a cold front produced one to three inches of rain across portions of central and eastern Virginia from Sunday night, March 28th, into Monday evening March 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts of one to three inches occurred across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts of one to three inches occurred across the county. Princess Anne reported 2.65 inches of rain.
